author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

In this book Augustin refutes the second letter which Petilianus wrote to him after having seen the first of Augustin’s earlier books. This letter had been full of violent language; and Augustin rather shows that the arguments of Petilianus had been deficient and irrelevant, than brings forward arguments in support of his own statements.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

This letter had been full of violent language; and Augustin rather shows that the arguments of Petilianus had been deficient and irrelevant, than brings forward arguments in support of his own statements.
title: Chapter 31

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: "And where," he says, "is the word that I added, wittingly?so that I did not say, He that has received his faith from one that is faithless; but, He that has received his faith wittingly from one that is faithless, receives not faith but guilt."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: He therefore who received his faith unwittingly from one that was faithless, received not guilt but faith; and accordingly I ask from what source he has received it?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And being thus placed in a strait, he answers, "He ought to have examined him."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Granted that he ought to have done so; but, as a matter of fact, he did not, or he was not able: what is your verdict about him?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Was he cleansed, or was he not?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If he was cleansed, I ask from what source?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For the polluted conscience of him that gave but not in holiness, with which he was unacquainted, could not cleanse him.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if he was not cleansed, command that he be so now.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You give no such orders, therefore he was cleansed.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Tell me by what means?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Do you at any rate tell me what Petilianus has failed to tell.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For I propose to you the very same words which he was unable to answer.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: "Behold the faithless man stands ready to baptize; but he who is to be baptized knows nothing of his faithlessness: what do you think that he will receive—faith, or guilt?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: This is sufficient as a constant form of question: answer, or search diligently to find what he has answered.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You will find abuse that has already been convicted.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: He finds fault with me, as though in derision, maintaining that I ought to suggest what is probable for consideration, since I cannot see the truth.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For, repeating my words, and cutting my sentence in two, he says, "Behold, you say, the faithless man stands ready to baptize; but he who is to be baptized knows nothing of his faithlessness."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Then he goes on to ask, "Who is the man, and from what corner has he started up, that you propose to us?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Just as though there were some one or two individuals, and such cases were not constantly occurring everywhere on either side!

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why does he ask of me who the man in question is, and from what corner he has started up, instead of looking round, and seeing that the churches are few and far between, whether in cities or in country districts, which do not contain men detected in crimes, and degraded from the ministry?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: While their true character was concealed, while they wished to be thought good, though really bad, and to be reputed chaste, though really guilty of adultery, so long they were involved in deceit; and so the Holy Spirit, according to the Scripture, was fleeing from them.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: It is from the crowd, therefore, of these men who hitherto concealed their character that the faithless man whom I suggested started up.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why does he ask me whence he started up, shutting his eyes to all this crowd, from which sufficient noise arises to satisfy the blind, if we take into consideration none but those who might have been convicted and degraded from their office?
